title: Brown Masoor Dal Tadka

overview.description: A hearty brown masoor dal cooked with onion, tomato and spices, then finished with a fragrant garlic and cumin tadka.

ingredients: 1 tbsp lemon juice, or to taste
method: Rinse the brown masoor dal thoroughly.
Soak the dal in plenty of water for 30–60 minutes if you have time.
Drain the dal.
Set the multi-cooker to sauté mode and add the oil and ghee.
Add the cumin seeds and cook until fragrant.
Add the onion and cook until softened and lightly golden.
Add the garlic, ginger and green chilli if using.
Cook for 1–2 minutes.
Add the chopped tomatoes, ground coriander, ground cumin, turmeric, Kashmiri chilli powder and salt.
Cook until the tomatoes have softened and the masala has thickened.
Add the drained masoor dal and stir to coat it in the masala.
Add the water and scrape the bottom of the pot thoroughly.
Cancel sauté mode and secure the lid.
Pressure cook on high pressure for 12 minutes.
Allow the pressure to release naturally for 10 minutes before releasing any remaining pressure.
Open the lid and stir the dal.
If necessary, use sauté mode to simmer the dal uncovered until it reaches the desired consistency.
Stir in the garam masala and lemon juice.
For the finishing tadka, heat the ghee in a small pan.
Add the cumin seeds.
When they begin to sizzle, add the sliced garlic and dried chilli.
Add the asafoetida if using and cook for a few seconds.
Cook until the garlic is lightly golden.
Pour the hot tadka over the dal.
Finish with fresh coriander and serve.

learning.did-you-know: Brown masoor dal is a whole or partially split lentil with its brown outer skin intact. Compared with red lentils, it has a firmer texture and a more earthy flavour.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How the outer skin of a pulse affects cooking time and texture, how to build a savoury masala and how a finishing tadka adds a second layer of flavour.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A hearty everyday dal that demonstrates another distinct pulse texture and gives the children a chance to compare three different forms of lentil and moong.
chapman-tips: Soaking is not essential but helps the dal cook more evenly.
Brown masoor should be tender but can retain a little more texture than red lentils.
Cook the onion until properly softened; this gives the dal a deeper savoury base.
Scrape the multi-cooker thoroughly before pressure cooking to reduce the risk of a burn warning.
The dal will thicken as it rests, so leave it slightly looser than your final desired consistency.
For less heat, omit the green chilli and keep the Kashmiri chilli powder for colour and flavour.
The tadka is worth doing even if the main dal is simple; it gives the finished dish a fresh, aromatic top note.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Cool completely before freezing in airtight portions. Thaw overnight in the refrigerator and reheat gently, adding water if necessary.
notes.leftovers: Thin with water and reheat as a soup, serve with rice or use as a filling for chapati or paratha.
